name: Three Sacred Treasures of Japan
shortDescription: The Three Sacred Treasures of Japan are a set of imperial regalia that symbolize the authority and legitimacy of the Emperor of Japan.
description: The Three Sacred Treasures of Japan (三種の神器, Sanshu no Jingi) are a set of three legendary artifacts that represent the Emperor of Japan and his divine right to rule. They are: the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙の剣), a legendary sword; the Yata no Kagami (八咫鏡), a mirror; and the Yasakani no Magatama (八尺瓊勾玉), a jewel. These treasures are deeply intertwined with Shinto mythology and imperial tradition, representing valor (the sword), wisdom (the mirror), and benevolence (the jewel), respectively. According to legend, the treasures were originally given to Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu, when he descended to Earth to establish the imperial line. The Kusanagi sword is said to have been found inside the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi by Susanoo, Amaterasu's brother. The Yata no Kagami and Yasakani no Magatama were crafted to lure Amaterasu out of the cave where she hid, plunging the world into darkness. The treasures are rarely seen in public and are kept in separate locations. The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is traditionally said to be housed at Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, although its presence there has never been definitively confirmed. The Yata no Kagami is kept at Ise Grand Shrine, the most sacred Shinto shrine, and the Yasakani no Magatama is kept at the Imperial Palace in Tokyo. The enthronement ceremony of a new emperor involves the symbolic presentation of these treasures, solidifying the emperor's connection to the divine and reinforcing the continuity of the imperial lineage. The treasures embody core values of Japanese culture and serve as powerful symbols of national identity and imperial authority. Their mythical origins and historical significance continue to resonate deeply within Japanese society.

sources.title: A Cultural History of Japanese Mythology
sources.author: Ueda, Masaaki
